--- SOURCE 3: The cognitive reality monitoring network and theories of ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117

SUMMARY:
Providing a clear definition of higher cognitive functions such as attention could be as difficult as for consciousness, yet there is consensus on attention (Lindsay, 2020, Petersen and Posner, 2012), and we can run animal experiments on attention with neural recordings and causal methods (Norman et al., 2021, Noudoost and Moore, 2011). Global neural ignition across the brain (Dehaene et al., 1998, Mashour et al., 2020), local recurrrent computations in sensory circuits (Lamme, 2006, Lamme, 2010), and self-monitoring of internal representations (Lau et al., 2022, Lau and Rosenthal, 2011), but remain relatively vague because they have no explicit link to the first level of understanding; the computational theory. The four main theories of consciousness (Michel et al., 2018) are the global neuronal workspace theory (GNW), local recurrent processing theory (RPT), higher-order theory (HOT), and integrated information theory (IIT).

FULL CONTENT:
The cognitive reality monitoring network and theories of consciousness - ScienceDirect

Review Article

The cognitive reality monitoring network and theories of consciousness
======================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Aurelio Cortese a, Mitsuo Kawato a b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neures.2024.01.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neures.2024.01.007 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0168010224000117&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Marr’s three levels of understanding can help the study of consciousness. 
*   •We define the prerequisites of consciousness within the CRMN framework. 
*   •The CRMN seeks to explain consciousness and metacognition through computational theory. 
*   •We discuss the neural architecture and computations of the CRMN. 
*   •We propose new experiments to test assumptions and predictions of the CRMN. 

Abstract
--------

[Theories of consciousness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/theory-of-consciousness "Learn more about Theories of consciousness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") abound. However, it is difficult to arbitrate reliably among competing theories because they target different levels of neural and [cognitive processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or anatomical loci, and only some were developed with computational models in mind. In particular, theories of consciousness need to fully address the three levels of understanding of the brain proposed by David Marr: computational theory, algorithms and hardware. Most major theories refer to only one or two levels, often indirectly. The cognitive reality monitoring network (CRMN) model is derived from computational theories of mixture-of-experts architecture, hierarchical reinforcement learning and generative/inference computing modules, addressing all three levels of understanding. A central feature of the CRMN is the mapping of a gating network onto the [prefrontal cortex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/prefrontal-cortex "Learn more about prefrontal cortex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), making it a prime coding circuit involved in monitoring the accuracy of one's mental states and distinguishing them from external reality. Because the CRMN builds on the hierarchical and layer structure of the [cerebral cortex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/cerebral-cortex "Learn more about cerebral cortex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), it may connect research and findings across species, further enabling concrete computational models of consciousness with new, explicitly testable hypotheses. In sum, we discuss how the CRMN model can help further our understanding of the nature and function of consciousness.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000129)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010223001669)

Keywords
--------

Computational theory of consciousness

Metacognition

Cognitive reality monitoring network

Marr’s levels

Representations

1. Introduction
---------------

The scientific study and understanding of consciousness is simultaneously a young research field and a long-standing philosophical ambition. There are many [theories of consciousness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/theory-of-consciousness "Learn more about theories of consciousness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [Seth & Bayne provide an excellent overview ([Seth and Bayne, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib85))], which differ from one another at the level of definitions, the focus on global versus local scales, and the underlying [neural circuits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neural-circuit "Learn more about neural circuits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Recent work has sought to define mechanisms and consensus for studying consciousness in biological and artificial systems ([Butlin et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib11)).

When we compare consciousness studies with other [cognitive neuroscience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/cognitive-neuroscience "Learn more about cognitive neuroscience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") fields, e.g. motor control, visual perception, memory, attention, and [perceptual learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/perceptual-learning "Learn more about perceptual learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), we hit upon three significant differences in research.

The first is related to the definition of consciousness. Providing a clear definition of higher cognitive functions such as attention could be as difficult as for consciousness, yet there is consensus on attention ([Lindsay, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib59), [Petersen and Posner, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib77)), and we can run animal experiments on attention with neural recordings and causal methods ([Norman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib72), [Noudoost and Moore, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib73)). It has proven challenging to run animal experiments on consciousness with the same level of rigour as with other cognitive functions. This difficulty primarily arises because consciousness has been classically described in relation to human subjective experience, and it is therefore hard to analyse in objective experiments applicable to other animals. We do not possess common experimental paradigms directly targeting consciousness in humans and experimental animals (but see ([Birch et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib6))).

The second difficulty may come from failing to explicitly address David Marr’s first level ([Marr, 1982](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib61)), the computational theory. What is consciousness for, how can its computation be made possible, and what are its inputs and outputs? To some extent, we can answer these questions for most brain functions, including motor control, visual perception, memory, attention, and [perceptual learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/perceptual-learning "Learn more about perceptual learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Although some theories suggest specific computations (see next section, _Definitions and theories of consciousness_), we are still missing a computational-level understanding of consciousness.

Finally, the third aspect concerns the computational models of David Marr's second and third levels. What are the algorithms, why could they be efficient with the computation aimed, and how are algorithms implemented in [neural substrates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/neural-substrate "Learn more about neural substrates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")? Some theories hint at possible algorithms or neural circuits as hardware. Global neural ignition across the brain ([Dehaene et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib20), [Mashour et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib62)), local recurrrent computations in sensory circuits ([Lamme, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib53), [Lamme, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib54)), and self-monitoring of internal representations ([Lau et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib58), [Lau and Rosenthal, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib57)), but remain relatively vague because they have no explicit link to the first level of understanding; the computational theory. Integrated Information Theory (IIT) instead has the form of a mathematical theory ([Oizumi et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib74), [Tononi, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib94)) but is far removed from David Marr’s three levels of understanding. IIT aims to achieve something similar to the Hamiltonian physics mechanics by the first principle. If it is valid, that will be transformative, but there have also been severe doubts about IIT, including invalid axioms, ad-hoc interpretations and changing assumptions ([Bayne, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib3), [Doerig et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib21), [Lau, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib56), [Morch, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib70)).

The cognitive reality monitoring network (CRMN) is different from these previous theories of consciousness in its origins. It is a computational model of neural processing mapping to a hierarchical-modular reinforcement learning architecture ([Kawato and Cortese, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib48)). Thus, the computational theory of CRMN is explicit with respect to David Marr's first level: to maximise ecological fitness through reinforcement learning (i.e., maximising some expected reward signal[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#fn1)). The CRMN algorithm is a hierarchical and modular reinforcement learning algorithm with multiple paired generative and inference models. CRMN solves sensory and motor control, perceptual processing, objective maximisation, and metacognition as subordinate functions. At the hardware level, CRMN allocates paired generative and inference models to the layer structure of the [cerebral cortex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/cerebral-cortex "Learn more about cerebral cortex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), reward prediction errors to the [basal ganglia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/basal-ganglion "Learn more about basal ganglia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and gains switching and cognitive reality monitoring to the PFC. A unique feature of CRMN is that consciousness was not the main objective of computational modelling. Metacognition and consciousness result from gating in hierarchical-modular reinforcement learning. That is, they both describe an inherent process of the model. As such, there is an explicit function for consciousness and metacognition in behavioural terms. In this sense, CRMN sits at the opposite extreme of IIT.

In the following sections, we will examine the current landscape of consciousness theories and how they map onto different levels of ontological representation. We will analyse the CRMN and its building blocks, providing an overview of its computations and how they can help our understanding of consciousness by linking it to other major consciousness theories. Within the CRMN framework, we will define the prerequisites for consciousness, such as a neural system with hierarchy, modularity, and information compression with representations chained over time. For an agent ‘acting’ in a dynamic, complex, non-stationary environment, consciousness as a coherent evolution in time of low-dimensional representations can provide an intrinsic tool for fast adaptive [behaviour](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/behavior-neuroscience "Learn more about behaviour from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Finally, we will discuss how the CRMN can be integrated into consciousness studies and help bridge the thorny question of how we can study theories of consciousness in animal models.

2. Definitions and theories of consciousness
--------------------------------------------

What is consciousness, and how do we define it? Typically, we equate consciousness with subjective experience. That is, a system (human, animal, or otherwise artificial) has a conscious experience when there is “something it is like” for the system to be the subject of that experience ([Butlin et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib11), [Nagel, 1974](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib71)).

The four main [theories of consciousness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/theory-of-consciousness "Learn more about theories of consciousness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Michel et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib66)) are the global neuronal workspace theory (GNW), local recurrent processing theory (RPT), higher-order theory (HOT), and integrated information theory (IIT). There are dozens more in practice, even though not all are as well known ([Seth and Bayne, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib85)). Some theories are widely accepted and seen as strong candidates to explain consciousness by the broader neuroscientific community (e.g., GNW, LR), while other theories are better known by the general public (e.g., IIT) and seen as more controversial in the field ([Fleming et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib25), [Lau, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib56), [Michel et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib66)).

We briefly highlight the key features of the four main theories. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#tbl0005) refers to these four theories and the CRMN, introducing the specifics for each of Marr’s three levels.
*   •GNW: global neuronal workspace (globalist view). Equates consciousness with conscious access, broadcasting information across cortical areas ([Dehaene et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib20), [Mashour et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib62)). For the GNW theory, the hallmark of consciousness is a “global ignition phenomenon, the sudden, coherent, and exclusive activation of a subset of workspace neurons coding for the current conscious content” (quoted from [Mashour et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib62)). For consciousness to arise, the theory proposes that neural [information processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/information-processing "Learn more about information processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has to travel from sensory areas to [prefrontal cortices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/prefrontal-cortex "Learn more about prefrontal cortices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and reverberate across the brain’s [neural networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/neural-network "Learn more about neural networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •RPT: recurrent processing theory (localist view). First-order representations generated through local recurrency are sufficient for consciousness ([Lamme, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib54)). Recurrent connections within and between [sensory processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/sensory-processing "Learn more about sensory processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") areas form the basis of conscious experience. Feedforward sweeps in recurrent networks will not be sufficient for consciousness, although they may be necessary. 
*   •HOT: higher-order theory of consciousness (meta-representations). More than mere first-order representations are required for consciousness to arise, for a first-order sensory representation may happen nonconsciously and determine behavioural responses but is not enough for phenomenal conscious experience. A second-order representation, or ‘meta-representation’, is necessary. Consciousness requires inner awareness from monitoring first-order states ([Brown et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib10), [Lau et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib58), [Lau and Rosenthal, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib57)). Several versions of the theory differ based on the nature of these second-order representations and the connection between first and second-order representations. 
*   •IIT: integrated information theory (theoretical-mathematical view). IIT identifies consciousness with a causal structure. Based on three axioms, IIT proposes that we can analytically calculate a measure (of integrated information) for any system from its causal structure. This measure directly reflects the ‘amount’ of consciousness in the system. IIT purports that recurrent systems are always conscious, while feedforward systems are never ([Oizumi et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib74), [Tononi, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib94)). 

Table 1. CRMN, mainstream theories of consciousness and how they map onto Marr’s three levels of understanding. CRMN: Cognitive reality monitoring network, GNW: Global neuronal workspace theory, RPT: Recurrent processing theory, HOT: Higher-order theory, IIT: Integrated information theory. *Note that while both GNW and HOT have an entry in the computational theory field, neither strictly describes the computational theory.

| Empty Cell | **Computational theory** | **Representation & algorithm** | **Hardware implementation** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **CRMN** | Maximise ecological fitness by reward-guided sensory-motor learning. For fast learning from small samples, dimension reduction is used through a divide-and-conquer strategy with hierarchy and modularity. | Fine to coarse representation hierarchy connected by generative/inference model pairs. Hierarchical and modular reinforcement learning algorithm based on representation hierarchy and modularity. | Generative and inference models are implemented as feedback and feedforward connections within cortical layer circuits. Basal ganglia as a reinforcement learning hub, and PFC for selection and switching of modules. |
| **GNW** | Solving the frame problem, i.e., * | Coherent activation of workspace neurons: broadcasting of information across cortical areas | Connections between sensory areas to prefrontal cortices |
| **RPT** | - | First-order representations through local recurrency | Recurrent connections within local networks |
| **HOT** | distinguish reliable from unreliable mental representations for belief formation and future behaviour * | Second-order or meta representation in PFC and first order representations in the lower areas are matched | Lower areas and PFC, and neural connections between them |
| **IIT** | - | - | - |

These summary definitions indicate that these theories do not necessarily address or formalise an overarching computational problem. Instead, they are concerned with explaining the nature of consciousness or the minimal set of neural underpinnings. Thus, each theory takes a different approach or targets different levels of Marr’s three levels of understanding of the brain ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#tbl0005)). To be fair, several researchers mention interesting characteristics of the computational aspects of GNW and HOT. In particular, Baars & Shanahan have argued that a global workspace architecture solves the frame problem in artificial intelligence ([Shanahan and Baars, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib86)). In the same way, for HOT, some researchers propose a computational-level goal: distinguish reliable from unreliable mental representations for belief formation and future [behaviour](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/behavior-neuroscience "Learn more about behaviour from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gershman, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib27), [Lau, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib55)).

Some theories offer insight into the neural hardware (sensory vs associative or frontal areas of the brain, whole brain networks), others into the algorithms (recurrence). Because of these differences and a need for standard definitions and computational building blocks, arbitrating among theories or falsifying predictions can be difficult and problematic. The difficulty of arbitrating between theories lies clear in recent adversarial collaborations ([Cogitate Consortium et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib15)). While these efforts are great for the field, they [face](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/face "Learn more about face from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") inherent problems when the theories tested need a robust common play level (in particular, [neural substrate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/neural-substrate "Learn more about neural substrate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and computations/representations).

Therefore, how can we bridge theories of consciousness and start validating or falsifying specific claims? One way we are advocating here is to use a framework that maps onto Marr’s three levels of analysis. Thus, to make within each theory explicit links with the computational theory (what computations underpin consciousness), the algorithms (what are the representations, what processes transform these representations, e.g., feedback for generative models, feedforward for inference models, prediction errors), and the neural hardware (e.g., cortical layers, circuits, regions).

3. The computational objective and prerequisites of consciousness
-----------------------------------------------------------------

What is consciousness’ function for survival? What is the input/output of conscious processes? What is the mechanism for its usefulness? These questions directly relate to Marr’s levels of understanding in that they lead us to think about the underlying computational theory.

We first suggest that consciousness is critical to creating relational content. That is to say, mental or neural content that is compositional and relational, as in an episode linking together different streams of information (e.g., context, actions, time, perception, etc.), combining sensory dimensions, [cognitive processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and levels of abstraction. At its core, and as advocated before ([Bengio, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib5), [Cortese et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010224000117#bib17)), consciousness could generate a low- or even uni-dimensional data point from very high-dimensional inputs. This way, over time, the brain can create a time series of low-dimensional data points (snapshots), amalgamating different sources of information into one coherent dimension.

One thing that results from this view is the actionability of such a construct. In short, a time series of low-dimensional representations can support complex behaviours without needing nonlinear transformations of high-dimensional data. However, to perform efficiently, and in light of recent work, the brain likely operates offline and online systems to manipulate the low-dimensional time series.

The online system can respond in real time to sensory information and the behavioural context in a changing, dynamic world. Sensorimotor, attention and [executive function](https:/

--- SOURCE 4: Defining Cognition Matters for Understanding Consciousness ---

###### [Cognition](/us/basics/cognition)

# Defining Cognition Matters for Understanding Consciousness

## A clearer picture of consciousness and its evolution may unlock its mystery.

Updated August 14, 2025

|
[Reviewed by Lybi Ma](/us/docs/editorial-process)

![Share on Facebook](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-facebook-md.svg)
![Share on X](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-twitter-md.svg)

![Share on LinkedIn](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-linkedin-md.svg)

### Key points

![eakgrungenerd / AdobeStock](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/article_inline_half_caption/public/field_blog_entry_images/2025-08/Image%20for%20blog%20-%20What%20Actually%20is%20Cognition%20-%20eakgrungenerd%20-%20AdobeStock.jpeg.jpg?itok=RuoI86UA "eakgrungenerd / AdobeStock")

The outcome of the 2024 U.S. presidential election, one of the most consequential in recent history, was determined in no small part by concerns about the cognitive state of President Biden and the way those concerns led others to pressure him to step down as the Democratic Party nominee at a late stage of the presidential race.

[Cognition](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/cognition "Psychology Today looks at Cognition") is one of the most frequently used terms concerning the brain and mental functioning. But what actually is it? Even experienced clinicians working in the field might have difficulty defining it precisely. There is not a single, universally agreed-upon definition.

Cognition is not exclusive to humans; it also occurs in many animals, and there is debate about whether or to what degree [artificial intelligence](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/artificial-intelligence "Psychology Today looks at artificial intelligence") qualifies as a cognitive system. In this five-part series, we will consider not only the question of what cognition is and how it can be impaired in humans, but also its gradations in different animals, how it evolved from simple building blocks, its relation to and implications for consciousness, and questions about engineering cognition and consciousness in AI.

## Cognitive deficits in humans

In humans, cognitive deficits—major or subtle, permanent or transient—are crucial to a great many conditions and mental states, including, for example, simple fatigue or drowsiness (causing poor [concentration](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/attention "Psychology Today looks at concentration")), intoxication, [medication](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/psychopharmacology "Psychology Today looks at medication") side effects, non-specific “[brain fog](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/brain-fog "Psychology Today looks at brain fog")” from a variety of medical conditions, [ADHD](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/adhd "Psychology Today looks at ADHD"), intellectual disability, brain injury, brain degeneration in Alzheimer’s and other forms of [dementia](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/dementia "Psychology Today looks at dementia"), acute delirium from a variety of causes, and major mental illnesses such as schizophrenia. The influential University of Cambridge professor of cognitive [neuroscience](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/neuroscience "Psychology Today looks at neuroscience"), Trevor Robbins, has argued that most, if not all, of the major neuropsychiatric disorders, such as schizophrenia, [depression](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/depression "Psychology Today looks at depression"), and [anxiety](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/anxiety "Psychology Today looks at anxiety"), are cognitive in nature.1

Cognition is routinely evaluated by clinicians using simple questions that assess, for example, orientation, concentration, [memory](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/memory "Psychology Today looks at memory"), ability to follow verbal or written instructions, and mental exercises such as requiring people to perform basic arithmetic calculations or to generate words starting with particular letters. It is also evaluated in much greater detail by psychologists using structured psychometric measures such as IQ tests and more specific neuropsychological tests for particular kinds of cognitive deficits.

## Defining cognition

## Unconscious processes

Frith tells us that cognition is considered one of three components of consciousness, alongside [emotion](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/emotions "Psychology Today looks at emotion") and will. He notes that it was once assumed that these processes were always conscious and that cognition could not occur without awareness. However, by the end of the 19th century, [Freud](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/freudian-psychology "Psychology Today looks at Freud") and Helmholtz (the latter more important and relevant to modern neuroscience) had formulated theories about [unconscious](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/unconscious "Psychology Today looks at unconscious") mental processes. They proposed that a significant portion of human behavior is guided by internal processes outside of conscious awareness. And by the mid-20th century, growing evidence began to support the view that most cognitive processing occurs without reaching conscious awareness.5

## Cognition versus emotion

Cognition has traditionally been differentiated from [emotion](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/finding-purpose/202408/what-actually-are-emotions), and this remains a useful general distinction, but one that is not as clear or categorical as it may seem. Cognition has typically referred to processes associated with conscious, deliberate thought; whereas emotion has been seen as affective (mood or feeling-related), automatic, bodily, and tied to value judgments (threat, reward, safety). But emotion and cognition are deeply intertwined and mutually shape each other. They also share overlapping neural networks. Furthermore, as noted, cognition can be unconscious and automatic. Also, emotion can include a cognitive evaluation component.

From an evolutionary point of view, emotion and cognition evolved together as adaptive functions. Emotions are fast, embodied evaluations of situations that help guide cognition and action. Cognitive functions evolved in part (and by some definitions) to predict, simulate, and regulate emotional responses—planning for future rewards or avoiding past dangers.

The distinction between cognition and emotion remains pragmatically useful but should not be viewed as reflecting fundamentally separate systems in the brain. They are best understood as deeply interwoven components of mental life, co-regulating behavior and experience across virtually all domains of functioning.

![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/field_blog_entry_images/2025-07/Paraknowledge.png.jpg?itok=uW_tli__ "ChatGPT modified by NostaLab.")
![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/teaser_image/blog_entry/2025-07/shutterstock_205256065.png.jpg?itok=8bUvZhqU "wavebreakmedia / Shutterstock ")

## Cognition versus intelligence

While cognition refers to the mental processes involved in acquiring, processing, storing, and using information, [intelligence](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/intelligence "Psychology Today looks at intelligence") refers to the capacity to apply cognitive processes effectively—to learn, adapt, solve problems, and make decisions across different situations. Cognition refers to the *how* of thinking; intelligence refers to the *effectiveness or efficiency* of cognitive functioning. Cognition is the tools and processes (memory, attention, reasoning); intelligence is how well those tools are used to solve problems or adapt to challenges.

## Cognition versus consciousness

Cognition, unlike consciousness, does not require subjective experience. Many forms of cognitive processing—[decision-making](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/decision-making "Psychology Today looks at decision-making") or [sensory integration](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/sensory-processing-disorder "Psychology Today looks at sensory integration")—can occur without awareness. Consciousness, on the other hand, *is* subjective experience or awareness—what it feels like to be aware of something, including thoughts, feelings, sensations, and the self. At the risk of oversimplifying, you might say that cognition is the processing engine of the mind, while consciousness is the experience of the light of awareness shining on some—but not all—those processes.

## Artificial intelligence

AI has been engineered to have various cognitive-like functions, and is increasingly intelligent, but at the time of writing, no forms of AI have come close to plausibly showing any remote signs of truly being conscious; however, large language models are extremely good at imitating human language and appearing conscious. It is not in principle impossible for future AIs to be conscious, but it remains a matter of debate as to what additional ingredientswould be required to achieve this.6 Furthermore, there is reasonably broad agreement that, for several reasons, it would be undesirable and ill-advised to do so, even if we knew how to design it. In Part 5 of this series, we will further explore the degree to which AI exhibits true cognition and the question of whether it could become conscious.

But first, in Parts 2 to 4, we will consider newer, sophisticated theories that propose deeper definitions of cognition. While there have been many attempts to define cognition more specifically, in this series, we will focus on theories that take an evolutionary perspective, and have a bearing on the relation between cognition and consciousness.7 Those theories have important implications for what it means for an animal or an AI to qualify as a cognitive system and, beyond that, as a conscious agent.

References

1. Trevor W. Robbins, “Cognition: The Ultimate Brain Function,” *Neuropsychopharmacology: Official Publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology* 36, no. 1 (2011): 1–2, <https://doi.org/10.1038/npp.2010.171>.

2. Wikipedia cites Oxford University Press and Dictionary.com for its definition. Cognitive processes use existing knowledge to discover new knowledge.* [Wikipedia, s.v. “Cognition,” accessed July 15, 2025, <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognition>]

3. George J. Augustine, Jennifer M. Groh, Scott A. Huettel, Anthony-Samuel LaMantia, Leonard E. White, and Dale Purves, eds., *Neuroscience*, 7th ed., chap. 27, “Cognitive Functions and the Organization of the Cerebral Cortex” (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2023), 712.

4. Christopher D. Frith, “Disorders of Conscious and Unconscious Mental Processes,” in *Principles of Neural Science*, 6th ed., ed. Eric R. Kandel, John D. Koester, Sarah H. Mack, and Steven A. Siegelbaum (New York: McGraw Hill, 2021), 1473.

5. Frith, “Cognition,” in *Principles of Neural Science*, 1473.

Webb, “What Is Cognition?” *Current Biology* 29, no. 13 (2019): R608–R615, <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2019.05.044>. [One of the 11 contributors to this paper, Nicky Clayton from the University of Cambridge, does take an evolutionary perspective and does study animal cognition].

enriched_points.expanded_content: Consciousness and cognition are intertwined; consciousness enables awareness of cognitive processes, influencing decision-making, sensory perception, and emotional responses, shaping our lived experiences.
Mental states serve critical functions, such as guiding behavior, facilitating decision-making, and enabling self-awareness, all of which are essential for adaptive responses in complex environments.

Notable cognitive theories include Global Neuronal Workspace Theory, which emphasizes widespread information access, and Higher-Order Thought Theory, which posits consciousness arises from meta-representations of mental states.
